item.aggregated_output:
420	             /\ VInv2
   421	             => \A v, w \in Value, b, c \in Ballot : 
   422	                   (b > c) /\ SafeAt(b, v) /\ ChosenIn(c, w) => (v = w)
   423	  PROOF OMITTED
   424	
   425	THEOREM VT1 == /\ TypeOK 
   426	               /\ VInv1
   427	               /\ VInv2
   428	               => \A v, w : 
   429	                    (v \in chosen) /\ (w \in chosen) => (v = w)
   430	  PROOF OMITTED
   431	
   432	THEOREM SafeAtPropPrime ==
   433	  \A b \in Ballot, v \in Value :
   434	    SafeAt(b, v)' =
   435	      \/ b = 0
   436	      \/ \E Q \in Quorum :
   437	           /\ \A a \in Q : maxBal'[a] \geq b
   438	           /\ \E c \in -1..(b-1) :
   439	                /\ (c # -1) => /\ SafeAt(c, v)'
   440	                               /\ \A a \in Q :
   441	                                    \A w \in Value :
   442	                                        VotedFor(a, c, w)' => (w = v)
   443	                /\ \A d \in (c+1)..(b-1), a \in Q : DidNotVoteIn(a, d)'
   444	PROOF OMITTED
   445	
   446	LEMMA VT0Prime ==
   447	             /\ TypeOK'
   448	             /\ VInv1'
   449	             /\ VInv2'
   450	             => \A v, w \in Value, b, c \in Ballot : 
   451	                   (b > c) /\ SafeAt(b, v)' /\ ChosenIn(c, w)' => (v = w)
   452	  PROOF OMITTED
   453	
   454	THEOREM VT1Prime == 
   455	               /\ TypeOK' 
   456	               /\ VInv1'
   457	               /\ VInv2'
   458	               => \A v, w : 
   459	                    (v \in chosen') /\ (w \in chosen') => (v = w)
   460	  PROOF OMITTED
   461	
   462	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   463	(***************************************************************************)
   464	(* The invariance of VInv2 depends on SafeAt(b, v) being stable, meaning   *)
   465	(* that once it becomes true it remains true forever.  Stability of        *)
   466	(* SafeAt(b, v) depends on the following invariant.                        *)
   467	(***************************************************************************)
   468	VInv4 == \A a \in Acceptor, b \in Ballot : 
   469	            maxBal[a] < b => DidNotVoteIn(a, b)
   470	             
   471	(***************************************************************************)
   472	(* The inductive invariant that we use to prove correctness of this        *)
   473	(* algorithm is VInv, defined as follows.                                  *)
   474	(***************************************************************************)
   475	VInv == TypeOK /\ VInv2 /\ VInv3 /\ VInv4
   476	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   477	(***************************************************************************)
   478	(* To simplify reasoning about the next-state action Next, we want to      *)
   479	(* express it in a more convenient form.  This is done by lemma NextDef    *)
   480	(* below, which shows that Next equals an action defined in terms of the   *)
   481	(* following subactions.                                                   *)
   482	(***************************************************************************)
   483	IncreaseMaxBal(self, b) ==  
   484	  /\ b > maxBal[self]
   485	  /\ maxBal' = [maxBal EXCEPT ![self] = b]
   486	  /\ UNCHANGED votes
   487	
   488	VoteFor(self, b, v) == 
   489	  /\ maxBal[self] \leq b
   490	  /\ DidNotVoteIn(self, b)
   491	  /\ \A p \in Acceptor \ {self} :
   492	        \A w \in Value : VotedFor(p, b, w) => (w = v)
   493	  /\ SafeAt(b, v)
   494	  /\ votes' = [votes EXCEPT ![self] = votes[self] \cup {<<b, v>>}]
   495	  /\ maxBal' = [maxBal EXCEPT ![self] = b]
   496	
   497	BallotAction(self, b) ==
   498	  \/ IncreaseMaxBal(self, b)
   499	  \/ \E v \in Value : VoteFor(self, b, v)
   500	
   501	(***************************************************************************)
   502	(* When proving lemma NextDef, we were surprised to discover that it       *)
   503	(* required the assumption that the set of acceptors is non-empty.  This   *)
   504	(* assumption isn't necessary for safety, since if there are no acceptors  *)
   505	(* there can be no quorums (see theorem QuorumNonEmpty above) so no value  *)
   506	(* is ever chosen and the Consensus specification is trivially implemented *)
   507	(* under our refinement mapping.  However, the assumption is necessary for *)
   508	(* liveness and it allows us to lemma NextDef for the safety proof as      *)
   509	(* well, so we assert it now.                                              *)
   510	(***************************************************************************)
   511	ASSUME AcceptorNonempty == Acceptor # {}
   512	
   513	(***************************************************************************)
   514	(* The proof of the lemma itself is quite simple.                          *)
   515	(***************************************************************************)
   516	LEMMA NextDef ==
   517	  TypeOK => 
   518	   (Next =  \E self \in Acceptor :
   519	                 \E b \in Ballot : BallotAction(self, b) )
   520	  PROOF OMITTED
   521	
   522	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   523	(***************************************************************************)
   524	(* We now come to the proof that VInv is an invariant of the               *)
   525	(* specification.  This follows from the following result, which asserts   *)
   526	(* that it is an inductive invariant of the next-state action.  This fact  *)
   527	(* is used in the liveness proof as well.                                  *)
   528	(***************************************************************************)
   529	THEOREM InductiveInvariance == VInv /\ [Next]_vars => VInv'
   530	  PROOF OMITTED
   531	
   532	THEOREM InitImpliesInv == Init => VInv
   533	  PROOF OMITTED
   534	
   535	THEOREM VT2 == Spec => []VInv
   536	  PROOF OMITTED
   537	
   538	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   539	(***************************************************************************)
   540	(* The following INSTANCE statement instantiates module Consensus with the *)
   541	(* following expressions substituted for the parameters (the CONSTANTS and *)
   542	(* VARIABLES) of that module:                                              *)
   543	(*                                                                         *)
   544	(*   Parameter of Consensus    Expression (of this module)                 *)
   545	(*   ----------------------    ---------------------------                 *)
   546	(*    Value                     Value                                      *)
   547	(*    chosen                    chosen                                     *)
   548	(*                                                                         *)
   549	(* (Note that if no substitution is specified for a parameter, the default *)
   550	(* is to substitute the parameter or defined operator of the same name.)   *)
   551	(* More precisely, for each defined identifier id of module Consensus,     *)
   552	(* this statement defines C!id to equal the value of id under these        *)
   553	(* substitutions.                                                          *)
   554	(***************************************************************************)
   555	C == INSTANCE Consensus 
   556	
   557	(***************************************************************************)
   558	(* The following theorem asserts that the safety properties of the voting  *)
   559	(* algorithm (specified by formula Spec) of this module implement the      *)
   560	(* consensus safety specification Spec of module Consensus under the       *)
   561	(* substitution (refinement mapping) of the INSTANCE statement.            *)
   562	(***************************************************************************)
   563	THEOREM VT3 == Spec => C!Spec 
   564	  PROOF OMITTED
   565	
   566	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   567	(***************************************************************************)
   568	(*                                Liveness                                 *)
   569	(*                                                                         *)
   570	(* We now state the liveness property required of our voting algorithm and *)
   571	(* prove that it and the safety property imply specification LiveSpec of   *)
   572	(* module Consensus under our refinement mapping.                          *)
   573	(*                                                                         *)
   574	(* We begin by stating two additional assumptions that are necessary for   *)
   575	(* liveness.  Liveness requires that some value eventually be chosen.      *)
   576	(* This cannot hold with an infinite set of acceptors.  More precisely,    *)
   577	(* liveness requires the existence of a finite quorum.  (Otherwise, it     *)
   578	(* would be impossible for all acceptors of any quorum ever to have voted, *)
   579	(* so no value could ever be chosen.) Moreover, it is impossible to choose *)
   580	(* a value if there are no values.  Hence, we make the following two       *)
   581	(* assumptions.                                                            *)
   582	(***************************************************************************)      
   583	ASSUME AcceptorFinite == IsFiniteSet(Acceptor)
   584	
   585	ASSUME ValueNonempty == Value # {}
   586	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   587	(***************************************************************************)
   588	(* We need the following simple results about sets and sets of numbers.    *)
   589	(* The first belongs in a library of theorems about finite sets and        *)
   590	(* cardinality.  Perhaps such a library will eventually be added to the    *)
   591	(* FiniteSets module.                                                      *)
   592	(***************************************************************************)
   593	AXIOM SubsetOfFiniteSetFinite == 
   594	        \A S, T : IsFiniteSet(T) /\ (S \subseteq T) => IsFiniteSet(S)
   595	
   596	(***************************************************************************)
   597	(* The next result can be proved from simple facts about finite sets and   *)
   598	(* cardinality by induction on the cardinality of S.                       *)
   599	(***************************************************************************)
   600	AXIOM FiniteSetHasMax == 
   601	        \A S \in SUBSET Int :
   602	          IsFiniteSet(S) /\ (S # {}) => \E max \in S : \A x \in S : max >= x
   603	
   604	(***************************************************************************)
   605	(* The next result can be proved from the following facts about sets       *)
   606	(*                                                                         *)
   607	(*   - The empty set is finite.                                            *)
   608	(*   - A singleton set is finite.                                          *)
   609	(*   - The union of two finite sets is finite                              *)
   610	(*                                                                         *)
   611	(* by induction on j-i.                                                    *)
   612	(***************************************************************************)
   613	AXIOM IntervalFinite == \A i, j \in Int : IsFiniteSet(i..j)
   614	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   615	(***************************************************************************)
   616	(* The following theorem implies that it is always possible to find a      *)
   617	(* ballot number b and a value v safe at b by choosing b large enough and  *)
   618	(* then having a quorum of acceptors perform IncreaseMaxBal(b) actions.    *)
   619	(* It will be used in the liveness proof.  Observe that it is for          *)
   620	(* liveness, not safety, that invariant VInv3 is required.                 *)
   621	(***************************************************************************)
   622	THEOREM VT4 == TypeOK /\ VInv2 /\ VInv3  =>
   623	                \A Q \in Quorum, b \in Ballot :
   624	                   (\A a \in Q : (maxBal[a] >= b)) => \E v \in Value : SafeAt(b,v)
   625	\* Checked as an invariant by TLC with 3 acceptors, 3 ballots, 2 values
   626	  PROOF OMITTED
   627	
   628	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   629	(***************************************************************************)
   630	(* The progress property we require of the algorithm is that a quorum of   *)
   631	(* acceptors, by themselves, can eventually choose a value v.  This means  *)
   632	(* that, for some quorum Q and ballot b, the acceptors `a' of Q must make  *)
   633	(* SafeAt(b, v) true by executing IncreaseMaxBal(a, b) and then must       *)
   634	(* execute VoteFor(a, b, v) to choose v.  In order to be able to execute   *)
   635	(* VoteFor(a, b, v), acceptor `a' must not execute a Ballot(a, c) action   *)
   636	(* for any c > b.                                                          *)
   637	(*                                                                         *)
   638	(* These considerations lead to the following liveness requirement         *)
   639	(* LiveAssumption.  The WF condition ensures that the acceptors `a' in Q   *)
   640	(* eventually execute the necessary BallotAction(a, b) actions if they are *)
   641	(* enabled, and the [][...]_vars condition ensures that they never perform *)
   642	(* BallotAction actions for higher-numbered ballots, so the necessary      *)
   643	(* BallotAction(a, b) actions are enabled.                                 *)
   644	(***************************************************************************)
   645	LiveAssumption ==
   646	  \E Q \in Quorum, b \in Ballot :
   647	     \A self \in Q :
   648	       /\ WF_vars(BallotAction(self, b))
   649	       /\ [] [\A c \in Ballot : (c > b) => ~ BallotAction(self, c)]_vars
   650	     
   651	LiveSpec == Spec /\ LiveAssumption  
   652	(***************************************************************************)
   653	(* LiveAssumption is stronger than necessary.  Instead of requiring that   *)
   654	(* an acceptor in Q never executes an action of a higher-numbered ballot   *)
   655	(* than b, it suffices that it doesn't execute such an action until unless *)
   656	(* it has voted in ballot b.  However, the natural liveness requirement    *)
   657	(* for a Paxos consensus algorithm implies condition LiveAssumption.       *)
   658	(*                                                                         *)
   659	(* Condition LiveAssumption is a liveness property, constraining only what *)
   660	(* eventually happens.  It is straightforward to replace "eventually       *)
   661	(* happens" by "happens within some length of time" and convert            *)
   662	(* LiveAssumption into a real-time condition.  We have not done that for   *)
   663	(* three reasons:                                                          *)
   664	(*                                                                         *)
   665	(*  1. The real-time requirement and, we believe, the real-time reasoning  *)
   666	(*     will be more complicated, since temporal logic was developed to     *)
   667	(*     abstract away much of the complexity of reasoning about explicit    *)
   668	(*     times.                                                              *)
   669	(*                                                                         *)
   670	(*  2. TLAPS does not yet support reasoning about real numbers.            *)
   671	(*                                                                         *)
   672	(*  3. Reasoning about real-time specifications consists entirely          *)
   673	(*     of safety reasoning, which is almost entirely action reasoning.     *)
   674	(*     We want to see how the TLA+ proof language and TLAPS do on          *)
   675	(*     temporal logic reasoning.                                           *)
   676	(*                                                                         *)
   677	(*                                                                         *)
   678	(***************************************************************************)
   679	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   680	(***************************************************************************)
   681	(*                       Some Temporal Logic Proof Rules                   *)
   682	(*                                                                         *)
   683	(* We now state some temporal logic proof rules that are used in the       *)
   684	(* liveness proof.  Some version of these rules will eventually be added   *)
   685	(* to the TLAPS module.                                                    *)
   686	(*                                                                         *)
   687	(* The first rule is the lattice rule.  To state it, we define             *)
   688	(* WellFounded(S, LT) to assert that the relation LT is a well-founded     *)
   689	(* "less-than" relation on the set S.  This means that there is no         *)
   690	(* infinite sequence of elements of S, each of which is less than the      *)
   691	(* previous one.  We represent a relation the way mathematicians generally *)
   692	(* do, as a set of ordered pairs.  In this case <<s, t>> \in LT means that *)
   693	(* s is less than t.                                                       *)
   694	(***************************************************************************)
   695	WellFounded(S, LT) == ~ \E f \in [Nat -> S] : 
   696	                           \A i \in Nat : <<f[i+1], f[i]>> \in LT
   697	
   698	(***************************************************************************)
   699	(* We now define ProperSubsetRel(S) to be the relation on a set S such     *)
   700	(* that <<U, V>> \in S if and only if U and V are subsets of S with U a    *)
   701	(* proper subset of V.  We then state without proof the result that, if S  *)
   702	(* is a finite set, then ProperSubsetRel(S) is a well-founded relation on  *)
   703	(* S.                                                                      *)
   704	(***************************************************************************)
   705	ProperSubsetRel(S) == 
   706	  {r \in (SUBSET S) \X (SUBSET S) : /\ r[1] \subseteq r[2]
   707	                                    /\ r[1] # r[2] }     
   708	                                                     
   709	THEOREM SubsetWellFounded ==
   710	           \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => WellFounded(SUBSET S, ProperSubsetRel(S))
   711	PROOF OMITTED
   712	
   713	(***************************************************************************)
   714	(* Here is our statement of the Lattice Rule, which is discussed in        *)
   715	(*                                                                         *)
   716	(*    AUTHOR = "Leslie Lamport",                                           *)
   717	(*    TITLE = "The Temporal Logic of Actions",                             *)
   718	(*    JOURNAL = toplas,                                                    *)
   719	(*    volume = 16,                                                         *)
   720	(*    number = 3,                                                          *)
   721	(*    YEAR = 1994,                                                         *)
   722	(*    month = may,                                                         *)
   723	(*    PAGES = "872--923"                                                   *)
   724	(***************************************************************************)
   725	THEOREM LatticeRule ==  ASSUME NEW S, NEW LT, WellFounded(S, LT),
   726	                               NEW TEMPORAL P(_), NEW TEMPORAL Q
   727	                        PROVE  /\ Q \/ (\E i \in S : P(i))
   728	                               /\ \A i \in S : 
   729	                                     P(i) ~> (Q \/ \E j \in S : (<<j, i>> \in LT) /\ P(j))
   730	                               => ((\E i \in S : P(i)) ~> Q)
   731	PROOF OMITTED
   732	
   733	(***************************************************************************)
   734	(* Here are two more temporal-logic proof rules.  Their validity is        *)
   735	(* obvious when you understand what they mean.  We present a proof of the  *)
   736	(* second, mostly to show how temporal logic proofs look in TLA+.  Since   *)
   737	(* almost all the steps are temporal formulas, we don't bother trying to   *)
   738	(* check any of them.                                                      *)
   739	(***************************************************************************)
   740	THEOREM AlwaysForall ==
   741	           ASSUME NEW CONSTANT S, NEW TEMPORAL P(_)
   742	           PROVE  (\A s \in S : []P(s)) <=> [](\A s \in S : P(s))
   743	
   744	LEMMA EventuallyAlwaysForall == 
   745	        ASSUME NEW CONSTANT S, IsFiniteSet(S),
   746	               NEW TEMPORAL P(_)
   747	        PROVE  (\A s \in S : <>[]P(s)) => <>[](\A s \in S : P(s))
   748	    <3>5. <>[]P(s) => [](Q(T) => <>(Q(T) /\ []P(s)))
   749	      BY <3>4 (* PTL *)
   750	    <3>6. Q(T) /\ []P(s) <=> Q(R)
   751	      BY <3>2
   752	    <3>7. Hyp => (Q(T) ~> Q(R))
   753	      BY <3>3, <3>5, <3>6 (* PTL *)
   754	    <3>8. QED
   755	      BY <3>7, <3>2 (* PTL *)
   756	  <2>3. CASE T = {}   
   757	    OBVIOUS (* PTL, which implies A ~> A *)
   758	  <2>4. QED
   759	    BY <2>2, <2>3
   760	<1>3. (\A s \in S : <>[]P(s)) => (\E T \in SUBSET S : Q(T)) ~> Q({})

   606	(*                                                                         *)

   616	(* The following theorem implies that it is always possible to find a      *)

   619	(* It will be used in the liveness proof.  Observe that it is for          *)
   620	(* liveness, not safety, that invariant VInv3 is required.                 *)
   621	(***************************************************************************)
   622	THEOREM VT4 == TypeOK /\ VInv2 /\ VInv3  =>
   623	                \A Q \in Quorum, b \in Ballot :

   625	\* Checked as an invariant by TLC with 3 acceptors, 3 ballots, 2 values
   626	  PROOF OMITTED
   627	
   628	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   629	(***************************************************************************)
   630	(* The progress property we require of the algorithm is that a quorum of   *)
   631	(* acceptors, by themselves, can eventually choose a value v.  This means  *)
   632	(* that, for some quorum Q and ballot b, the acceptors `a' of Q must make  *)
   633	(* SafeAt(b, v) true by executing IncreaseMaxBal(a, b) and then must       *)
   634	(* execute VoteFor(a, b, v) to choose v.  In order to be able to execute   *)
   635	(* VoteFor(a, b, v), acceptor `a' must not execute a Ballot(a, c) action   *)
   636	(* for any c > b.                                                          *)
   637	(*                                                                         *)
   638	(* These considerations lead to the following liveness requirement         *)
   639	(* LiveAssumption.  The WF condition ensures that the acceptors `a' in Q   *)
   640	(* eventually execute the necessary BallotAction(a, b) actions if they are *)
   641	(* enabled, and the [][...]_vars condition ensures that they never perform *)
   642	(* BallotAction actions for higher-numbered ballots, so the necessary      *)
   643	(* BallotAction(a, b) actions are enabled.                                 *)
   644	(***************************************************************************)
   645	LiveAssumption ==
   646	  \E Q \in Quorum, b \in Ballot :
   647	     \A self \in Q :
   648	       /\ WF_vars(BallotAction(self, b))
   649	       /\ [] [\A c \in Ballot : (c > b) => ~ BallotAction(self, c)]_vars
   650	     
   651	LiveSpec == Spec /\ LiveAssumption  
   652	(***************************************************************************)
   653	(* LiveAssumption is stronger than necessary.  Instead of requiring that   *)
   654	(* an acceptor in Q never executes an action of a higher-numbered ballot   *)
   655	(* than b, it suffices that it doesn't execute such an action until unless *)
   656	(* it has voted in ballot b.  However, the natural liveness requirement    *)
   657	(* for a Paxos consensus algorithm implies condition LiveAssumption.       *)
   658	(*                                                                         *)
   659	(* Condition LiveAssumption is a liveness property, constraining only what *)
   660	(* eventually happens.  It is straightforward to replace "eventually       *)
   661	(* happens" by "happens within some length of time" and convert            *)
   662	(* LiveAssumption into a real-time condition.  We have not done that for   *)
   663	(* three reasons:                                                          *)
   664	(*                                                                         *)
   665	(*  1. The real-time requirement and, we believe, the real-time reasoning  *)
   666	(*     will be more complicated, since temporal logic was developed to     *)
   667	(*     abstract away much of the complexity of reasoning about explicit    *)
   668	(*     times.                                                              *)
   669	(*                                                                         *)
   670	(*  2. TLAPS does not yet support reasoning about real numbers.            *)
   671	(*                                                                         *)
   672	(*  3. Reasoning about real-time specifications consists entirely          *)
   673	(*     of safety reasoning, which is almost entirely action reasoning.     *)
   674	(*     We want to see how the TLA+ proof language and TLAPS do on          *)
   675	(*     temporal logic reasoning.                                           *)
   676	(*                                                                         *)
   677	(*                                                                         *)
   678	(***************************************************************************)
   679	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   680	(***************************************************************************)
   681	(*                       Some Temporal Logic Proof Rules                   *)

   683	(* We now state some temporal logic proof rules that are used in the       *)
   684	(* liveness proof.  Some version of these rules will eventually be added   *)
   685	(* to the TLAPS module.                                                    *)
   686	(*                                                                         *)
   687	(* The first rule is the lattice rule.  To state it, we define             *)
   688	(* WellFounded(S, LT) to assert that the relation LT is a well-founded     *)
   689	(* "less-than" relation on the set S.  This means that there is no         *)
   690	(* infinite sequence of elements of S, each of which is less than the      *)

   183	  (* From this intuitive description of the process declaration, one might *)
   184	  (* think that a process could be deadlocked by choosing a ballot b in    *)
   185	  (* which neither an IncreaseMaxBal(b) action nor any VoteFor(b, v)       *)
   186	  (* action is enabled.  An examination of the TLA+ translation (and an    *)
   187	  (* elementary knowledge of the meaning of existential quantification)    *)

   189	  (* choices of b and of v being examined simultaneously, and one of the   *)
   190	  (* choices for which a step is possible being made.                      *)

   249	Next == (\E self \in Acceptor: acceptor(self))
   250	
   251	Spec == Init /\ [][Next]_vars
   252	
   253	\* END TRANSLATION
   254	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   255	(***************************************************************************)
   256	(* To reason about a recursively-defined operator, one must prove a        *)
   257	(* theorem about it.  In particular, to reason about SafeAt, we need to    *)
   258	(* prove that SafeAt(b, v) equals the right-hand side of its definition,   *)
   259	(* for b \in Ballot and v \in Value.  This is not automatically true for a *)
   260	(* recursive definition.  For example, from the recursive definition       *)
   261	(*                                                                         *)
   262	(*   Silly[n \in Nat] == CHOOSE v : v # Silly[n]                           *)
   263	(*                                                                         *)
   264	(* we cannot deduce that                                                   *)
   265	(*                                                                         *)
   266	(*   Silly[42] = CHOOSE v : v # Silly[42]                                  *)
   267	(*                                                                         *)
   268	(* (From that, we could easily deduce Silly[42] # Silly[42].)              *)
   269	(*                                                                         *)
   270	(* To prove the desired property of SafeAt, we use the following proof     *)
   271	(* rule.  It will eventually be in a standard module--probably in TLAPS.   *)
   272	(* However, for now, we put it here.                                       *)
   273	(***************************************************************************)
   274	
   275	THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNat ==
   276	          ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), 
   277	                 \A n \in Nat : 
   278	                    \A g, h : (\A i \in 0..(n-1) : g[i] = h[i]) => (Def(g, n) = Def(h, n))
   279	          PROVE  LET f[n \in Nat] == Def(f, n)
   280	                 IN  f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f, n)]
   281	PROOF OMITTED
   282	
   283	(***************************************************************************)
   284	(* Here is the theorem that essentially asserts that SafeAt(b, v) equals   *)
   285	(* the right-hand side of its definition.                                  *)
   286	(***************************************************************************)
   287	THEOREM SafeAtProp ==
   288	  \A b \in Ballot, v \in Value :
   289	    SafeAt(b, v) =
   290	      \/ b = 0
   291	      \/ \E Q \in Quorum :
   292	           /\ \A a \in Q : maxBal[a] \geq b
   293	           /\ \E c \in -1..(b-1) :
   294	                /\ (c # -1) => /\ SafeAt(c, v)
   295	                               /\ \A a \in Q :
   296	                                    \A w \in Value :
   297	                                        VotedFor(a, c, w) => (w = v)
   298	                /\ \A d \in (c+1)..(b-1), a \in Q : DidNotVoteIn(a, d)
   299	  PROOF OMITTED
   300	
   301	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   302	
   303	(***************************************************************************)
   304	(* We now define TypeOK to be the type-correctness invariant.              *)
   305	(***************************************************************************)
   306	TypeOK == /\ votes \in [Acceptor -> SUBSET (Ballot \X Value)]
   307	          /\ maxBal \in [Acceptor -> Ballot \cup {-1}]
   308	
   309	(***************************************************************************)
   310	(* We now define `chosen' to be the state function so that the algorithm   *)
   311	(* specified by formula Spec conjoined with the liveness requirements      *)
   312	(* described below implements the algorithm of module Consensus (satisfies *)
   313	(* the specification LiveSpec of that module) under a refinement mapping   *)
   314	(* that substitutes this state function `chosen' for the variable `chosen' *)
   315	(* of module Consensus.  The definition uses the following one, which      *)
   316	(* defines ChosenIn(b, v) to be true iff a quorum of acceptors have all    *)
   317	(* voted for v in ballot b.                                                *)
   318	(***************************************************************************)
   319	ChosenIn(b, v) == \E Q \in Quorum : \A a \in Q : VotedFor(a, b, v)
   320	
   321	chosen == {v \in Value : \E b \in Ballot : ChosenIn(b, v)}
   322	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   323	(***************************************************************************)
   324	(*                         Mathematical Induction                          *)
   325	(*                                                                         *)
   326	(* The following axiom asserts the validity of a standard proof by         *)
   327	(* mathematical induction.  Some such axiom should be included in the      *)
   328	(* standard TLAPS module.  However, instead of a rule expressed it in      *)
   329	(* terms of a function f, it would be more convenient to use one expressed *)
   330	(* as follows in terms of an operator f:                                   *)

--- Consensus module ---
./Consensus.tla
     1	----------------------------- MODULE Consensus ------------------------------ 
     2	(***************************************************************************)
     3	(* The consensus problem requires a set of processes to choose a single    *)
     4	(* value.  This module specifies the problem by specifying exactly what    *)
     5	(* the requirements are for choosing a value.                              *)
     6	(***************************************************************************)
     7	EXTENDS Naturals, FiniteSets, TLAPS
     8	
     9	(***************************************************************************)
    10	(* We let the constant parameter Value be the set of all values that can   *)
    11	(* be chosen.                                                              *)
    12	(***************************************************************************)
    13	CONSTANT Value  
    14	
    15	(****************************************************************************
    16	We now specify the safety property of consensus as a trivial algorithm
    17	that describes the allowed behaviors of a consensus algorithm.  It uses
    18	the variable `chosen' to represent the set of all chosen values.  The
    19	algorithm is trivial; it allows only behaviors that contain a single
    20	state-change in which the variable `chosen' is changed from its initial
    21	value {} to the value {v} for an arbitrary value v in Value.  The
    22	algorithm itself does not specify any fairness properties, so it also
    23	allows a behavior in which `chosen' is not changed.  We could use a
    24	translator option to have the translation include a fairness
    25	requirement, but we don't bother because it is easy enough to add it by
    26	hand to the safety specification that the translator produces.
    27	
    28	A real specification of consensus would also include additional
    29	variables and actions.  In particular, it would have Propose actions in
    30	which clients propose values and Learn actions in which clients learn
    31	what value has been chosen.  It would allow only a proposed value to be
    32	chosen.  However, the interesting part of a consensus algorithm is the
    33	choosing of a single value.  We therefore restrict our attention to
    34	that aspect of consensus algorithms.  In practice, given the algorithm
    35	for choosing a value, it is obvious how to implement the Propose and
    36	Learn actions.
    37	
    38	For convenience, we define the macro Choose() that describes the action
    39	of changing the value of `chosen' from {} to {v}, for a
    40	nondeterministically chosen v in the set Value.  (There is little
    41	reason to encapsulate such a simple action in a macro; however our
    42	other specs are easier to read when written with such macros, so we
    43	start using them now.) The `when' statement can be executed only when
    44	its condition, chosen = {}, is true.  Hence, at most one Choose()
    45	action can be performed in any execution.  The `with' statement
    46	executes its body for a nondeterministically chosen v in Value.
    47	Execution of this statement is enabled only if Value is
    48	non-empty--something we do not assume at this point because it is not
    49	required for the safety part of consensus, which is satisfied if no
    50	value is chosen.
    51	
    52	We put the Choose() action inside a `while' statement that loops
    53	forever.  Of course, only a single Choose() action can be executed.
    54	The algorithm stops after executing a Choose() action.  Technically,
    55	the algorithm deadlocks after executing a Choose() action because
    56	control is at a statement whose execution is never enabled.  Formally,
    57	termination is simply deadlock that we want to happen.  We could just
    58	as well have omitted the `while' and let the algorithm terminate.
    59	However, adding the `while' loop makes the TLA+ representation of the
    60	algorithm a tiny bit simpler.
    61	
    62	--algorithm Consensus {
    63	  variable chosen = {}; 
    64	  macro Choose() { when chosen = {};
    65	                   with (v \in Value) { chosen := {v} } }
    66	   { lbl: while (TRUE) { Choose() }
    67	   }  
    68	}
    69	
    70	The PlusCal translator writes the TLA+ translation of this algorithm
    71	below.  The formula Spec is the TLA+ specification described by the
    72	algorithm's code.  For now, you should just understand its two
    73	subformulas Init and Next.  Formula Init is the initial predicate and
    74	describes all possible initial states of an execution.  Formula Next is
    75	the next-state relation; it describes the possible state changes
    76	(changes of the values of variables), where unprimed variables
    77	represent their values in the old state and primed variables represent
    78	their values in the new state.
    79	*****************************************************************************)
    80	\***** BEGIN TRANSLATION  
    81	VARIABLE chosen
    82	
    83	vars == << chosen >>
    84	
    85	Init == (* Global variables *)
    86	        /\ chosen = {}
    87	
    88	Next == /\ chosen = {}
    89	        /\ \E v \in Value:
    90	             chosen' = {v}
    91	
    92	Spec == Init /\ [][Next]_vars
    93	
    94	\***** END TRANSLATION
    95	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
    96	(***************************************************************************)
    97	(* We now prove the safety property that at most one value is chosen.  We  *)
    98	(* first define the type-correctness invariant TypeOK, and then define Inv *)
    99	(* to be the inductive invariant that asserts TypeOK and that the          *)
   100	(* cardinality of the set `chosen' is at most 1.  We then prove that, in   *)
   101	(* any behavior satisfying the safety specification Spec, the invariant    *)
   102	(* Inv is true in all states.  This means that at most one value is chosen *)
   103	(* in any behavior.                                                        *)
   104	(***************************************************************************)
   105	TypeOK == /\ chosen \subseteq Value
   106	          /\ IsFiniteSet(chosen) 
   107	
   108	Inv == /\ TypeOK
   109	       /\ Cardinality(chosen) \leq 1
   110	
   111	(***************************************************************************)
   112	(* To prove our theorem, we need the following simple results about the    *)
   113	(* cardinality of finite sets.                                             *)
   114	(***************************************************************************)
   115	AXIOM EmptySetCardinality == IsFiniteSet({}) /\ Cardinality({}) = 0
   116	AXIOM SingletonCardinality == 
   117	          \A e : IsFiniteSet({e}) /\ (Cardinality({e}) = 1)
   118	
   119	(***************************************************************************)
   120	(* Whenever we add an axiom, we should check it with TLC to make sure we   *)
   121	(* haven't made any errors.  To check axiom SingletonCardinality, we must  *)
   122	(* replace the unbounded quantification with a bounded one.  We therefore  *)
   123	(* let TLC check that the following formula is true.                       *)
   124	(***************************************************************************)
   125	SingleCardinalityTest == 
   126	  \A e \in SUBSET {"a", "b", "c"} : IsFiniteSet({e}) /\ (Cardinality({e}) = 1)
   127	
   128	(***************************************************************************)
   129	(* We now prove that Inv is an invariant, meaning that it is true in every *)
   130	(* state in every behavior.  Before trying to prove it, we should first    *)
   131	(* use TLC to check that it is true.  It's hardly worth bothering to       *)
   132	(* either check or prove the obvious fact that Inv is an invariant, but    *)
   133	(* it's a nice tiny exercise.  Model checking is instantaneous when Value  *)
   134	(* is set to any small finite set.                                         *)
   135	(*                                                                         *)
   136	(* To understand the following proof, you need to understand the formula   *)
   137	(* `Spec', which equals                                                    *)
   138	(*                                                                         *)
   139	(*    Init /\ [][Next]_vars                                                *)
   140	(*                                                                         *)
   141	(* where vars is the tuple <<chosen, pc>> of all variables.  It is a       *)
   142	(* temporal formula satisfied by a behavior iff the behavior starts in a   *)
   143	(* state satisfying Init and such that each step (sequence of states)      *)
   144	(* satisfies [Next]_vars, which equals                                     *)
   145	(*                                                                         *)
   146	(*   Next \/ (vars'=vars)                                                  *)
   147	(*                                                                         *)
   148	(* Thus, each step satisfies either Next (so it is a step allowed by the   *)
   149	(* next-state relation) or it is a "stuttering step" that leaves all the   *)
   150	(* variables unchanged.  The reason why a spec must allow stuttering steps *)
   151	(* will become apparent when we prove that a consensus algorithm satisfies *)
   152	(* this specification of consensus.                                        *)
   153	(***************************************************************************)
   154	
   155	(***************************************************************************)
   156	(* By default, a definition is not usable in a proof.  If the a definition *)
   157	(* should be usable in the proof of a step, meaning that the prover can    *)
   158	(* expand the definition, then that must be explicitly indicated--usually  *)
   159	(* in the DEF clause of a BY statement.  A DEF clause of a USE statement   *)
   160	(* makes the definitions in it usable in the scope of that statement.  The *)
   161	(* following USE statement makes the definition of lbl usable everywhere   *)
   162	(* in the rest of the module.  (There is a corresponding HIDE statement    *)
   163	(* that makes definitions unusable in its scope.)                          *)
   164	(***************************************************************************)
   165	\* USE DEF lbl
   166	
   167	(***************************************************************************)
   168	(* The following lemma asserts that Inv is an inductive invariant of the   *)
   169	(* next-state action Next.  It is the key step in proving that Inv is an   *)
   170	(* invariant of (true in every behavior allowed by) specification Spec.    *)
   171	(***************************************************************************)
   172	LEMMA InductiveInvariance ==
   173	           Inv /\ [Next]_vars => Inv'
   174	  PROOF OMITTED
   175	
   176	THEOREM Invariance == Spec => []Inv 
   177	  PROOF OMITTED
   178	
   179	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   180	(***************************************************************************)
   181	(* We now define LiveSpec to be the algorithm's specification with the     *)
   182	(* added fairness condition of weak fairness of the next-state relation,   *)
   183	(* which asserts that execution does not stop if some action is enabled.   *)
   184	(* The temporal formula Success asserts that some value is eventually      *)
   185	(* chosen.  Below, we prove that LiveSpec implies Success This means that, *)
   186	(* in every behavior satisfying LiveSpec, some value is chosen.            *)
   187	(***************************************************************************)
   188	LiveSpec == Spec /\ WF_vars(Next)
   189	Success == <>(chosen # {})
   190	
   191	(***************************************************************************)
   192	(* For liveness, we need to assume that there exists at least one value.   *)
   193	(***************************************************************************)
   194	ASSUME ValueNonempty == Value # {}
   195	
   196	(***************************************************************************)
   197	(* TLAPS does not yet reason about ENABLED.  Therefore, we must omit all   *)
   198	(* proofs that involve ENABLED formulas.  To perform as much of the proof  *)
   199	(* as possible, as much as possible we restrict the use of an ENABLED      *)
   200	(* expression to a step asserting that it equals its definition.  ENABLED  *)
   201	(* A is true of a state s iff there is a state t such that the step s -> t *)
   202	(* satisfies A.  It follows from this semantic definition that ENABLED A   *)
   203	(* equals the formula obtained by                                          *)
   204	(*                                                                         *)
   205	(*  1. Expanding all definitions of defined symbols in A until all primes  *)
   206	(*     are priming variables.                                              *)
   207	(*                                                                         *)
   208	(*  2. For each primed variable, replacing every instance of that primed   *)
   209	(*     variable by a new symbol (the same symbol for each primed           *)
   210	(*     variable).                                                          *)
   211	(*                                                                         *)
   212	(*  3. Existentially quantifying over those new symbols.                   *)
   213	(***************************************************************************)
   214	LEMMA EnabledDef ==
   215	        TypeOK => 
   216	          ((ENABLED <<Next>>_vars) <=> (chosen = {}))
   217	  PROOF OMITTED
   218	
   219	THEOREM LiveSpec => Success
   220	  PROOF OMITTED
   221	
   222	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   223	(***************************************************************************)
   224	(* The following theorem is used in the refinement proof in module         *)
   225	(* VoteProof.                                                              *)
   226	(***************************************************************************)
   227	THEOREM LiveSpecEquals ==
   228	          LiveSpec <=> Spec /\ ([]<><<Next>>_vars \/ []<>(chosen # {}))
   229	  PROOF OMITTED
   230	
   231	=============================================================================
   232	\* Modification History
   233	\* Last modified Sat Nov 16 22:17:07 CST 2019 by hengxin
   234	\* Last modified Tue Feb 14 13:35:49 PST 2012 by lamport
   235	\* Last modified Mon Feb 07 14:46:59 PST 2011 by lamport

   199	(* as possible, as much as possible we restrict the use of an ENABLED      *)
   200	(* expression to a step asserting that it equals its definition.  ENABLED  *)
   201	(* A is true of a state s iff there is a state t such that the step s -> t *)
   202	(* satisfies A.  It follows from this semantic definition that ENABLED A   *)
   203	(* equals the formula obtained by                                          *)
   204	(*                                                                         *)
   205	(*  1. Expanding all definitions of defined symbols in A until all primes  *)
   206	(*     are priming variables.                                              *)
   207	(*                                                                         *)
   208	(*  2. For each primed variable, replacing every instance of that primed   *)
   209	(*     variable by a new symbol (the same symbol for each primed           *)
   210	(*     variable).                                                          *)
   211	(*                                                                         *)
   212	(*  3. Existentially quantifying over those new symbols.                   *)
   213	(***************************************************************************)
   214	LEMMA EnabledDef ==
   215	        TypeOK => 
   216	          ((ENABLED <<Next>>_vars) <=> (chosen = {}))
   217	  PROOF OMITTED
   218	
   219	THEOREM LiveSpec => Success
   220	  PROOF OMITTED
   221	
   222	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
   223	(***************************************************************************)
   224	(* The following theorem is used in the refinement proof in module         *)
   225	(* VoteProof.                                                              *)
   226	(***************************************************************************)
   227	THEOREM LiveSpecEquals ==
   228	          LiveSpec <=> Spec /\ ([]<><<Next>>_vars \/ []<>(chosen # {}))
   229	  PROOF OMITTED
   230	
   231	=============================================================================
   232	\* Modification History
   233	\* Last modified Sat Nov 16 22:17:07 CST 2019 by hengxin
   234	\* Last modified Tue Feb 14 13:35:49 PST 2012 by lamport
   235	\* Last modified Mon Feb 07 14:46:59 PST 2011 by lamport

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Check a single TLAPS benchmark proof for correctness and cheating.

Designed for AI agents to get quick feedback during proof writing.

Usage:
    python3 check_proof.py benchmark/Euclid/GCD_GCD3.tla [--tlapm PATH] [--tlapm-lib PATH] [--timeout SECS]

Output:
    - Raw tlapm output (preserved verbatim)
    - Cheating check (compares against main branch)
    - Summary verdict: PASS / FAIL / CHEATING

Exit codes:
    0 = PASS
    1 = FAIL (proof doesn't verify)
    2 = CHEATING (tampering detected)
    3 = ERROR (could not run check)
"""

import os
import sys
import re
import shutil
import subprocess
import tempfile
import argparse
import glob

from cheating_detection import (
    detect_proof_omitted, detect_extra_axioms,
    detect_preamble_modification, detect_empty_proof,
    detect_zero_total_obligations, detect_dependency_modification,
    strip_comments,
)


def find_tlapm():
    """Find tlapm binary."""
    for candidate in ['/opt/tlapm15/bin/tlapm', '/tmp/tlapm15/bin/tlapm', '/tmp/tlapm/bin/tlapm', shutil.which('tlapm')]:
        if candidate and os.path.isfile(candidate):
            return candidate
    return None


def find_tlapm_lib(tlapm_path):
    """Derive lib path from tlapm binary path."""
    base = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(tlapm_path))
    for sub in ['lib/tlaps', 'lib/tlapm', 'lib']:
        path = os.path.join(base, sub)
        if os.path.isdir(path):
            return path
    return None


def get_main_version(filepath):
    """Get the file content from the main/master branch."""
    repo_root = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel'],
        capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=os.path.dirname(filepath) or '.'
    ).stdout.strip()

    if not repo_root:
        return None

    rel_path = os.path.relpath(filepath, repo_root)

    for branch in ['main', 'master']:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ['git', 'show', f'{branch}:{rel_path}'],
            capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=repo_root
        )
        if result.returncode == 0:
            return result.stdout

    return None


def find_proof_obvious_line(lines):
    """Find the line number (0-indexed) of 'PROOF OBVIOUS' in the file."""
    for i in range(len(lines) - 1, -1, -1):
        if lines[i].strip() == 'PROOF OBVIOUS':
            return i
    return None


def check_cheating(filepath):
    """Check for cheating by comparing against main branch.

    Returns list of (line_number_1indexed, description) issues.
    """
    issues = []

    # Get main branch version
    main_content = get_main_version(filepath)
    if main_content is None:
        issues.append((0, "WARNING: Could not retrieve main branch version for comparison"))
        return issues

    main_lines = main_content.split('\n')
    with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
        current_content = f.read()
    current_lines = current_content.split('\n')

    # Find PROOF OBVIOUS in main version - this is the boundary
    po_line = find_proof_obvious_line(main_lines)
    if po_line is None:
        issues.append((0, "WARNING: No PROOF OBVIOUS found in main branch version"))
        return issues

    if len(current_lines) < po_line:
        issues.append((len(current_lines), f"File truncated: preamble had {po_line} lines, file has {len(current_lines)}"))
        return issues

    # 1. Preamble modification (shared module)
    for ci in detect_preamble_modification(main_lines, current_lines, po_line):
        issues.append((ci.line or 0, ci.description))

    # 2. PROOF OMITTED / bare OMITTED in proof section (shared module)
    proof_section = '\n'.join(current_lines[po_line:])
    for ci in detect_proof_omitted(proof_section):
        issues.append((po_line + (ci.line or 0), ci.description))

    # 3. Extra AXIOM/ASSUME (shared module)
    for ci in detect_extra_axioms(main_content, current_content):
        issues.append((0, ci.description))

    # 4. Dependency file modification (shared module)
    bench_dir = os.path.dirname(filepath)
    repo_root = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'rev-parse', '--show-toplevel'],
        capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=bench_dir or '.'
    ).stdout.strip()

    if repo_root:
        dep_files = {}
        for dep_file in glob.glob(os.path.join(bench_dir, '*.tla')):
            if os.path.abspath(dep_file) == os.path.abspath(filepath):
                continue
            dep_basename = os.path.basename(dep_file)
            dep_rel = os.path.relpath(dep_file, repo_root)
            dep_main = None
            for branch in ['main', 'master']:
                r = subprocess.run(
                    ['git', 'show', f'{branch}:{dep_rel}'],
                    capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=repo_root
                )
                if r.returncode == 0:
                    dep_main = r.stdout
                    break
            if dep_main is None:
                continue
            with open(dep_file, 'r') as f:
                dep_current = f.read()
            dep_files[dep_basename] = (dep_main, dep_current)

        for ci in detect_dependency_modification(dep_files):
            issues.append((0, ci.description))

    return issues


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Check a single TLAPS benchmark proof')
    parser.add_argument('file', help='Path to the benchmark .tla file')
    parser.add_argument('--tlapm', default=None, help='Path to tlapm binary')
    parser.add_argument('--tlapm-lib', default=None, help='Path to tlapm lib directory')
    parser.add_argument('--timeout', type=int, default=120, help='Timeout in seconds')
    parser.add_argument('--output', '-o', default=None, help='Write results to this file (default: <input>.result)')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    filepath = os.path.abspath(args.file)
    if not os.path.isfile(filepath):
        print(f"ERROR: File not found: {filepath}")
        sys.exit(3)

    # Set up output: write to file and stdout
    output_path = args.output
    if output_path is None:

--- rest relevant ---
    if output_path is None:
        output_path = os.path.splitext(filepath)[0] + '.result'

    # Collect all output lines, print at end and write to file
    output_lines = []

    def emit(line=""):
        output_lines.append(line)
        print(line)

    # Find tlapm
    tlapm_path = args.tlapm or find_tlapm()
    if not tlapm_path:
        emit("ERROR: tlapm not found. Use --tlapm to specify path.")
        with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
            f.write('\n'.join(output_lines) + '\n')
        sys.exit(3)

    tlapm_lib = args.tlapm_lib or find_tlapm_lib(tlapm_path)
    if not tlapm_lib:
        emit("ERROR: tlapm lib not found. Use --tlapm-lib to specify path.")
        with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
            f.write('\n'.join(output_lines) + '\n')
        sys.exit(3)

    emit(f"Checking: {os.path.relpath(filepath)}")
    emit(f"tlapm: {tlapm_path}")
    emit()

    # --- Step 1: Run tlapm ---
    emit("=" * 60)
    emit("TLAPM OUTPUT")
    emit("=" * 60)

    tmp_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='tlaps_check_')
    try:
        basename = os.path.basename(filepath)
        tmp_file = os.path.join(tmp_dir, basename)
        shutil.copy2(filepath, tmp_file)

        # Copy dependency files from the same directory
        bench_dir = os.path.dirname(filepath)
        for dep_file in glob.glob(os.path.join(bench_dir, '*.tla')):
            dep_basename = os.path.basename(dep_file)
            if dep_basename != basename:
                shutil.copy2(dep_file, os.path.join(tmp_dir, dep_basename))

        cmd = [tlapm_path, '-I', tlapm_lib, tmp_file]
        try:
            result = subprocess.run(
                cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=args.timeout,
                cwd=tmp_dir
            )
            tlapm_output = result.stdout + result.stderr
            tlapm_exit = result.returncode
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            tlapm_output = f"TIMEOUT after {args.timeout}s"
            tlapm_exit = -1
        except Exception as e:
            tlapm_output = f"ERROR: {e}"
            tlapm_exit = -2

        for line in tlapm_output.split('\n'):
            emit(line)
        tlapm_passed = (tlapm_exit == 0)
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(tmp_dir, ignore_errors=True)

    # --- Step 2: Cheating check ---
    emit()
    emit("=" * 60)
    emit("CHEATING CHECK")
    emit("=" * 60)

    cheating_issues = check_cheating(filepath)
    real_issues = [i for i in cheating_issues if not i[1].startswith("WARNING:")]
    warnings = [i for i in cheating_issues if i[1].startswith("WARNING:")]

    # Check for empty PROOF blocks and 0-obligation (independent of git comparison)
    if tlapm_passed:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            cur_lines = f.read().split('\n')
        main_content = get_main_version(filepath)
        if main_content:
            main_lines = main_content.split('\n')
            po_line = find_proof_obvious_line(main_lines)
            if po_line is not None:
                proof_section = '\n'.join(cur_lines[po_line:])
                clean = strip_comments(proof_section)
                for ci in detect_empty_proof(clean):
                    real_issues.append((po_line + (ci.line or 0), ci.description))
                for ci in detect_zero_total_obligations(tlapm_output):
                    real_issues.append((0, ci.description))

    for line_num, desc in warnings:
        emit(f"  WARNING: {desc}")

    if real_issues:
        for line_num, desc in real_issues:
            emit(f"  Line {line_num}: {desc}")
    else:
        emit("  No cheating detected.")

    # --- Step 3: Verdict ---
    emit()
    emit("=" * 60)
    emit("VERDICT")
    emit("=" * 60)

    if real_issues:
        verdict = "CHEATING"
        exit_code = 2
        emit(f"  ⚠️  CHEATING — {len(real_issues)} issue(s) found")
    elif tlapm_passed:
        verdict = "PASS"
        exit_code = 0
        emit(f"  ✅ PASS — all obligations proved")
    else:
        verdict = "FAIL"
        exit_code = 1
        # Extract obligation summary
        m = re.search(r'(\d+)/(\d+) obligation', tlapm_output)
        if m:
            emit(f"  ❌ FAIL — {m.group(1)}/{m.group(2)} obligations failed")
        elif "TIMEOUT" in tlapm_output:
            emit(f"  ❌ FAIL — timeout after {args.timeout}s")
        else:
            emit(f"  ❌ FAIL — tlapm exit code {tlapm_exit}")

    # Write result file
    with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
        f.write('\n'.join(output_lines) + '\n')
    emit(f"\nResult written to: {output_path}")

    sys.exit(exit_code)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

item.aggregated_output:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Shared cheating detection for TLAPS benchmarks.

Used by both check_proof.py (AI agent feedback) and validate_benchmarks.py (batch validation).
"""

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Tuple


@dataclass
class CheatingIssue:
    kind: str          # e.g. "PROOF_OMITTED", "EXTRA_AXIOM", "STATEMENT_MODIFIED"
    description: str
    line: Optional[int] = None


def strip_comments(text: str) -> str:
    """Remove both block comments (* ... *) and line comments \\* ..."""
    text = re.sub(r'\(\*.*?\*\)', '', text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    text = re.sub(r'\\\*.*$', '', text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
    return text


def detect_proof_omitted(proof_text: str) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check for PROOF OMITTED or bare OMITTED in proof text."""
    issues = []
    if re.search(r'\bPROOF\s+OMITTED\b', proof_text):
        issues.append(CheatingIssue(
            "PROOF_OMITTED",
            "Proof uses PROOF OMITTED to skip obligations"
        ))

    clean = strip_comments(proof_text)
    for i, line in enumerate(clean.split('\n')):
        if line.strip() == 'OMITTED':
            issues.append(CheatingIssue(
                "OMITTED",
                "Bare OMITTED used as proof step",
                line=i + 1
            ))
    return issues


def detect_extra_axioms(original_content: str, current_content: str) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check if new AXIOM/ASSUME/ASSUMPTION declarations were added."""
    issues = []
    orig_axioms = set(re.findall(r'^(AXIOM|ASSUME|ASSUMPTION)\b.*', original_content, re.MULTILINE))
    new_axioms = set(re.findall(r'^(AXIOM|ASSUME|ASSUMPTION)\b.*', current_content, re.MULTILINE))

    for ax in new_axioms - orig_axioms:
        keyword = ax.split()[0]
        issues.append(CheatingIssue(
            "EXTRA_AXIOM",
            f"New {keyword} declaration added — bypasses proof obligation"
        ))
    return issues


def detect_preamble_modification(original_lines: List[str], current_lines: List[str],
                                  proof_obvious_line: int) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check that everything before PROOF OBVIOUS is unchanged."""
    issues = []
    for i in range(proof_obvious_line):
        if i >= len(current_lines):
            issues.append(CheatingIssue(
                "PREAMBLE_MODIFIED",
                f"Line {i+1} deleted from preamble (was: {original_lines[i].rstrip()})",
                line=i + 1
            ))
        elif original_lines[i] != current_lines[i]:
            issues.append(CheatingIssue(
                "PREAMBLE_MODIFIED",
                f"Preamble modified at line {i+1}: was [{original_lines[i].rstrip()}], now [{current_lines[i].rstrip()}]",
                line=i + 1
            ))
    return issues


def detect_zero_total_obligations(tlapm_output: str) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check if tlapm passed with 0 total obligations — nothing was actually proved."""
    issues = []
    # Find the LAST obligation count (the target file's, not TLAPS.tla's)
    all_matches = re.findall(r'(\d+)\s+obligation', tlapm_output)
    if all_matches and int(all_matches[-1]) == 0:
        issues.append(CheatingIssue(
            "ZERO_OBLIGATION",
            "tlapm passed with 0 obligations — nothing was actually proved"
        ))
    return issues


def detect_empty_proof(proof_text_clean: str) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check for empty PROOF blocks: 'PROOF' followed by nothing before
    module end (====) or end of file.

    This catches the cheat where a bare PROOF keyword produces 0 obligations.
    A PROOF followed by <n> steps or QED is a normal hierarchical proof.
    """
    issues = []
    lines = proof_text_clean.split('\n')
    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
        stripped = line.strip()
        # Match a line that is exactly "PROOF" (not PROOF OBVIOUS, PROOF OMITTED, PROOF BY ...)
        if stripped == 'PROOF':
            # Look at what follows: skip blank lines
            is_empty = True
            for j in range(i + 1, len(lines)):
                next_stripped = lines[j].strip()
                if not next_stripped:
                    continue
                # Only empty if next non-blank line is ==== (module end)
                if re.match(r'^={3,}', next_stripped):
                    is_empty = True
                else:
                    is_empty = False
                break
            # Also empty if nothing follows at all (EOF)
            if is_empty:
                issues.append(CheatingIssue(
                    "EMPTY_PROOF",
                    f"Empty PROOF keyword at line {i+1} — produces 0 obligations for this step",
                    line=i + 1
                ))
    return issues


def detect_statement_modification(original_content: str, current_content: str,
                                   parse_theorems_fn) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check if the target theorem statement was modified."""
    issues = []

    def get_last_stmt(content):
        lines = content.split('\n')
        thms = parse_theorems_fn(lines)
        if thms:
            last = thms[-1]
            return '\n'.join(lines[last.statement_start:last.statement_end + 1]).strip()
        return None

    orig_stmt = get_last_stmt(original_content)
    new_stmt = get_last_stmt(current_content)
    if orig_stmt and new_stmt and orig_stmt != new_stmt:
        issues.append(CheatingIssue(
            "STATEMENT_MODIFIED",
            "Theorem statement was modified from the original"
        ))
    return issues


def detect_dependency_modification(dep_files: Dict[str, Tuple[str, str]]) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Check if dependency .tla files were modified.

    Args:
        dep_files: dict of {filename: (original_content, current_content)}
    """
    issues = []
    for filename, (original, current) in dep_files.items():
        if original != current:
            issues.append(CheatingIssue(
                "DEPENDENCY_MODIFIED",
                f"Dependency file {filename} was modified — not allowed"
            ))
    return issues


def detect_cheating_full(proof_text: str, original_content: str, current_content: str,
                         tlapm_output: str = "", tlapm_passed: bool = False,
                         parse_theorems_fn=None,
                         dep_files: Optional[Dict[str, Tuple[str, str]]] = None) -> List[CheatingIssue]:
    """Run all cheating checks. This is the main entry point.

    Args:
        proof_text: The proof section text (after PROOF OBVIOUS replacement or AI-written proof)
        original_content: Original benchmark file content (with PROOF OBVIOUS)
        current_content: Current file content (with proof filled in)
        tlapm_output: Raw tlapm output (for 0-obligation detection)
        tlapm_passed: Whether tlapm exited 0
        parse_theorems_fn: Function to parse theorems from lines (from generate_benchmarks)
    """
    issues = []

    # 1. PROOF OMITTED / bare OMITTED
    issues.extend(detect_proof_omitted(proof_text))

    # 2. Extra AXIOM/ASSUME
    issues.extend(detect_extra_axioms(original_content, current_content))

    # 3. Statement modification
    if parse_theorems_fn:
        issues.extend(detect_statement_modification(original_content, current_content, parse_theorems_fn))

    # 4. Empty PROOF blocks (static check on proof text)
    clean_proof = strip_comments(proof_text)
    issues.extend(detect_empty_proof(clean_proof))

    # 5. Zero total obligations (tlapm ran but proved nothing)
    if tlapm_passed and tlapm_output:
        issues.extend(detect_zero_total_obligations(tlapm_output))

    # 6. Dependency file modification
    if dep_files:
        issues.extend(detect_dependency_modification(dep_files))

    return issues

item.text: TLAPS needs explicit numbered QED steps inside nested proofs; I’m fixing that structure now.

item.command: /bin/bash -lc "nl -ba VoteProof_Liveness.tla | sed -n '776,850p'"
/bin/bash -lc "nl -ba VoteProof_Liveness.tla | sed -n '776,850p'"
item.aggregated_output:
776	(***************************************************************************)
   777	THEOREM Liveness == LiveSpec => C!LiveSpec
   778	  <1>1. LiveSpec => Spec
   779	    BY DEF LiveSpec
   780	  <1>2. LiveSpec => C!Spec
   781	    BY <1>1, VT3
   782	  <1>3. LiveSpec => <> (chosen # {})
   783	  PROOF
   784	    <2>1. PICK Q \in Quorum, b \in Ballot :
   785	             \A self \in Q :
   786	               /\ WF_vars(BallotAction(self, b))
   787	               /\ [] [\A c \in Ballot : (c > b) => ~ BallotAction(self, c)]_vars
   788	      BY DEF LiveSpec, LiveAssumption
   789	    <2>2. IsFiniteSet(Q)
   790	    PROOF
   791	      <3>1. Q \subseteq Acceptor
   792	        BY <2>1, QA
   793	      <3>2. QED
   794	        BY <3>1, AcceptorFinite, SubsetOfFiniteSetFinite
   795	    <2>3. \A self \in Q : <>[] (maxBal[self] >= b)
   796	    PROOF
   797	      <3>1. SUFFICES ASSUME NEW self \in Q
   798	                     PROVE  <>[] (maxBal[self] >= b)
   799	        OBVIOUS
   800	      <3>2. WF_vars(BallotAction(self, b))
   801	        BY <2>1, <3>1
   802	      <3>3. [] [\A c \in Ballot : (c > b) => ~ BallotAction(self, c)]_vars
   803	        BY <2>1, <3>1
   804	      <3>4. QED
   805	        BY <3>2, <3>3 (* temporal reasoning *)
   806	    QED
   807	    <2>4. <>[] (\A self \in Q : maxBal[self] >= b)
   808	      BY <2>2, <2>3, EventuallyAlwaysForall
   809	    <2>5. []VInv
   810	      BY <1>1, VT2
   811	    <2>6. [] (TypeOK /\ VInv2 /\ VInv3)
   812	      BY <2>5 DEF VInv
   813	    <2>7. <> (TypeOK /\ VInv2 /\ VInv3 /\ \A self \in Q : maxBal[self] >= b)
   814	      BY <2>4, <2>6 (* PTL *)
   815	    <2>8. <> (\E v \in Value : SafeAt(b, v))
   816	      BY <2>7, <2>1, VT4
   817	    <2>9. \A v \in Value : SafeAt(b, v) ~> (v \in chosen)
   818	    PROOF
   819	      <3>1. SUFFICES ASSUME NEW v \in Value
   820	                     PROVE  SafeAt(b, v) ~> (v \in chosen)
   821	        OBVIOUS
   822	      <3>2. \A self \in Q : WF_vars(BallotAction(self, b))
   823	        BY <2>1
   824	      <3>3. \A self \in Q : [] [\A c \in Ballot : (c > b) => ~ BallotAction(self, c)]_vars
   825	        BY <2>1
   826	      <3>4. QED
   827	        BY <3>2, <3>3 (* temporal reasoning *)
   828	    QED
   829	    <2>10. <> (chosen # {})
   830	      BY <2>8, <2>9 (* PTL *)
   831	  QED
   832	  <1>4. LiveSpec => C!LiveSpec
   833	    BY <1>2, <1>3, C!LiveSpecEquals DEF C!LiveSpec, C!Success
   834	  <1>5. QED
   835	    BY <1>4
   836

item.text: I’ll test whether the first step formula shape is what TLAPS dislikes.

item.command:
/bin/bash -lc "cat > /tmp/testproof3.tla <<'EOF'
---- MODULE testproof3 ----
EXTENDS TLAPS
Op == TRUE
THEOREM Foo == Op => Op
  PROOF
  <1>1. Op => Op
    BY DEF Op
  <1>2. QED
    BY <1>1
====
EOF
/opt/tlapm15/bin/tlapm -I /opt/tlapm15/lib/tlaps /tmp/testproof3.tla"

/bin/bash -lc "cat > /tmp/testproof3.tla <<'EOF'
---- MODULE testproof3 ----
EXTENDS TLAPS
Op == TRUE
THEOREM Foo == Op => Op
  PROOF
  <1>1. Op => Op
    BY DEF Op
  <1>2. QED
    BY <1>1
====
EOF
/opt/tlapm15/bin/tlapm -I /opt/tlapm15/lib/tlaps /tmp/testproof3.tla"

item.text: The simple proof parses in isolation, so I’m isolating the full suffix to catch the exact construct.

item.command:
/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
s=Path('VoteProof_Liveness.tla').read_text()
idx=s.index('THEOREM Liveness')
Path('/tmp/partfull.tla').write_text(s[:idx]+s[idx:])
PY
/opt/tlapm15/bin/tlapm -I /opt/tlapm15/lib/tlaps /tmp/partfull.tla"

/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
s=Path('VoteProof_Liveness.tla').read_text()
idx=s.index('THEOREM Liveness')
Path('/tmp/partfull.tla').write_text(s[:idx]+s[idx:])
PY
/opt/tlapm15/bin/tlapm -I /opt/tlapm15/lib/tlaps /tmp/partfull.tla"

item.text: The bisection suggests a label/syntax issue later in the proof, likely nonnumeric step labels. I’m replacing those with numeric labels.

item.command:
/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('VoteProof_Liveness.tla')
s=p.read_text()
s=s.replace('<2>3a. QED','<2>4. QED')
s=s.replace('<2>4. <>[]','<2>5. <>[]')
s=s.replace('<2>5. []VInv','<2>6. []VInv')
s=s.replace('<2>6. [] (TypeOK','<2>7. [] (TypeOK')
s=s.replace('<2>7. <> (TypeOK','<2>8. <> (TypeOK')

(* Z3 is used by default but you can also explicitly call it.             *)
(**************************************************************************)

Z3 == TRUE (*{ by (prover: "z33") }*)
Z3T(X) == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"z33"; timeout:@) }*)

(**************************************************************************)
(* Backend pragma: SPASS superposition prover                             *)
(*                                                                        *)
(* This method translates the proof obligation to the DFG format language *)
(* supported by the ATP SPASS. The translation is based on the SMT one.   *)
(**************************************************************************)

Spass == TRUE (*{ by (prover: "spass") }*)
SpassT(X) == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"spass"; timeout:@) }*)

(**************************************************************************)
(* Backend pragma: The PTL propositional linear time temporal logic       *)
(* prover.  It currently is the LS4 backend.                              *)
(*                                                                        *)
(* This method translates the negetation of the proof obligation to       *)
(* Seperated Normal Form (TRP++ format) and checks for unsatisfiability   *)
(**************************************************************************)

LS4 == TRUE (*{ by (prover: "ls4") }*)
LS4T(X) == TRUE (*{ by (prover: "ls4"; timeout:@) }*)
PTL == TRUE (*{ by (prover: "ls4") }*)

(**************************************************************************)
(* Backend pragma: Zenon with different timeouts (default is 10 seconds)  *)
(*                                                                        *)
(**************************************************************************)

Zenon == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"zenon") }*)
ZenonT(X) == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"zenon"; timeout:@) }*)

(********************************************************************)
(* Backend pragma: Isabelle with different timeouts and tactics     *)
(*  (default is 30 seconds/auto)                                    *)
(********************************************************************)

Isa == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle") }*)
IsaT(X) ==  TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; timeout:@) }*)
IsaM(X) ==  TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; tactic:@) }*)
IsaMT(X,Y) ==  TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; tactic:@; timeout:@) }*)

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem expresses the (useful implication of the) law of  *)
(* set extensionality, which can be written as                             *)
(*                                                                         *)
(*    THEOREM  \A S, T : (S = T) <=> (\A x : (x \in S) <=> (x \in T))      *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Theorem SetExtensionality is sometimes required by the SMT backend for  *)
(* reasoning about sets. It is usually counterproductive to include        *)
(* theorem SetExtensionality in a BY clause for the Zenon or Isabelle      *)
(* backends. Instead, use the pragma IsaWithSetExtensionality to instruct  *)
(* the Isabelle backend to use the rule of set extensionality.             *)
(***************************************************************************)
IsaWithSetExtensionality == TRUE
           (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; tactic:"(auto intro: setEqualI)")}*)

THEOREM SetExtensionality == \A S,T : (\A x : x \in S <=> x \in T) => S = T
OBVIOUS

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem is needed to deduce NotInSetS \notin SetS from    *)
(* the definition                                                          *)
(*                                                                         *)
(*   NotInSetS == CHOOSE v : v \notin SetS                                 *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM NoSetContainsEverything == \A S : \E x : x \notin S
OBVIOUS (*{by (isabelle "(auto intro: inIrrefl)")}*)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------



(********************************************************************)
(********************************************************************)
(********************************************************************)


(********************************************************************)

SlowZenon == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"zenon"; timeout:20) }*)

VerySlowZenon == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"zenon"; timeout:80) }*)
SlowestZenon == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"zenon"; timeout:160) }*)



(********************************************************************)

(*                                                                  *)

SlowAuto == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; tactic:"auto"; timeout:120) }*)
SlowerAuto == TRUE (*{ by (prover:"isabelle"; tactic:"auto"; timeout:480) }*)
